comment_content:
Check his review.

My opinion: Firstly the labs are absolute dogshit. I did not learn anything and I never had to use my brain. The only hard part is understanding the syntax of scala and the new library being used. Every lab you have to go through the documentation of some stupid ass super specific library.

As for the course itself, I think the course is ok. Not super interesting. The only interesting thing to me was LL1 parsing, nothing else.
comment_teaching:
Monotonous. Lectures notes also have errors sometimes.

What makes me give this a C (which is rare) is the fact that the lectures notes are arranged in bullet points and everything is so specific you can't find it on the internet many times. For example, say you're trying to understand type checking. The lectures notes do not elaborate, they just gives the answer to the questions. If you try to find it on the internet, you'll get many unrelated things.

Not being able to search it on the internet along with summarized notes makes me give this a C.
comment_workload:
Workload is ok. As mentioned before, the labs may take time because you basically have to go through 2 different documentations for each lab.

As a closing remark, I'd say I think this course is for a specific audience. I see some people fairly enjoyed this course and I do not relate to them at all. For example, the other guy who has posted the review - maybe he likes these kind of things more (or maybe he's just rating it high because he got 90/100 :P )
comment_grading:
Projects: 60%

Five minor projects worth 12% each

One optional project which can make up for at most half of the marks lost in the mini-projects

Exam: 40% Letter grade

Everyone gets nearly full in labs so your grade is determined by the final exam.

Mean: 44.24
Median: 39 High: 90
Upper Quartile: 49 Low: 11
Lower Quartile: 32

The final exam is kind of shitty. For example in the last question you had to fucking read 2 pages of code and understand their super specific scenario and then implement it in both amy (the language we coded) and wasm. I don't remember anything about amy...

I mean just look at the statistics, you'll understand.

This is why in my opinion this course just feels like getting used to the syntax rather than really using your brain. I have to say he makes some nice questions, but it really overshadowed by other negative aspects.

Mine final : 46, grade = B. So 3 marks away from UQ gets you a B - as you can see, very concentrated and not very generous.

comment_content:
Previous code: COMP 4901U (https://ust.space/review/COMP4901U).

4 Major Parts: Lexing, Parsing, Type Checking and Lowering (Code Generation). All of them are fundamental to compilers.

Although the "official" prerequisites are COMP 3021 or 3031, I suggest taking the following before you take the course:

- COMP 2611. This is for code generation.
- COMP 2711. This is for necessary discrete math basics. Proofs, Induction and Sets.
- COMP 3031. This is for the Scala basics and a broader view on programming languages, which are what we want to compile! Moreover, there are some overlapping that will make your life easier.

Believe it or not. Just enjoy the content.
comment_teaching:
Professor PL teaches in a decent way, just like the recursive descent parser. I think he did explain all concepts that he wanted to present, but some of them didn't appear in a clear way in the lecture notes, (e.g., LL(1) Parser). I suggest you directly asking Professor PL or the TAs as they are willing to answer your question.

Edit: Oh no! I forgot to mention our kindest TAs!

In this semester Anto and Tony were the TAs for grading assignments/exams and presenting tutorials. Anto was mainly presenting the tutorial and Tony sometimes were invisible (I've heard that he went to Japan quite often).

The tutorial materials are really well-organized and clear; doing them is more helpful than doing past papers. In the first few tutorials, there were many exercises that couldn't be done in one tutorial section. The rest tutorials are great as the TAs actively saw our progress and put the question that we hadn't yet solved to the next tutorial.

The tutorials really helped a lot in understanding the materials. I'd suggest all CSE courses to introduce such tutorials to solve exercises. The tutorial exercises are usually more difficult than the final exam, so if you managed to understand all of them, then your final exam should be good.
comment_workload:
It deserves your time. Put time on it. Review it and understand it.

The projects usually take a few hours (3-4 hours) to finish, resulting in 9/10 marks. If you want to get 10/10, then probably you'll have to spend more time on it.
comment_grading:
5 Projects: 60%
1 Exam: 40%

An optional that can make up up to 50% of the marks lost in the projects.

The projects are simple and trivial in the sense that the median is 9-10/10 and the UQ is always 10/10. Although I always have some marks deducted. I got:

Project 1: 10/10
Project 2: 9/10
Project 3: 9/10
Project 4: 9/10
Project 5: 9.5/10

You can always make up the marks lost with the optional project, so don't worry too much about the projects.

As people usually get high marks in the projects, the exam is the place where students are "differentiated." Don't be scared by the difficulty because if you don't understand the question, usually everyone neither understand.

In this semester, it seemed that the exam was too difficult, as Professor PL sent an announcement after the exam, saying

> Don't worry, we realize that this exam was unexpectedly more difficult than usual and will make corresponding adjustments.

Low: 11/100
LQ: 32/100
Median: 39/100
UQ: 49/100
High: 90/100
Mean: 44.24/100

Me: 90/100. Alright, it seemed that I pulled up the mean 🤕🤫. TBH
